coredata.dc:title: Tunable oxygen activation induced by oxygen defects in nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots for sustainable boosting photocatalysis
coredata.dc:creator: Di, Jun
Xia, Jiexiang
Chen, Xiaoliu
Ji, Mengxia
Yin, Sheng
Zhang, Qi
Li, Huaming
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)/BiPO4 materials have been prepared through an ionic liquid assisted solvothermal method. After the introduction of N-CQDs, the N-CQDs/BiPO4 materials exhibited the increased photocatalytic activity for the degradation of four kinds of colorless pollutants under UV irradiation, such as antibiotic ciprofloxacin, enrofloxacin, tetracycline and phenol 4-chlorophenol. Different from roles of carbon quantum dots previous reported, the improved photocatalytic activity was mainly attributed to the molecular oxygen activation ability of N-CQDs, which derived from the oxygen defects on the surface of N-CQDs. The N-CQDs/BiPO4 materials displayed excellent reusability and stability and demonstrated the oxygen defects in N-CQDs could sustainable activate molecular oxygen for organic pollutant removal under UV irradiation. By the electron spin resonance analysis, the main active species was determined to be superoxide radicals rather than hydroxyl radicals. This study presented some new insight for the mode of action of N-CQDs for the increased photocatalytic activity and also provided an approach to enhance the molecular oxygen activation for photocatalytic pollutant removal or selective organic synthesis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns for pure BiPO4 and N-CQDs/BiPO4 samples with different N-CQDs contents. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a–c) TEM images and (d) HRTEM image of N-CQDs/BiPO4-1 samples.
Steady-state PL spectra of pure BiPO4 and N-CQDs/BiPO4-1 materials. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Photocatalytic degradation of CIP in the presence of pure BiPO4 and N-CQDs/BiPO4 materials under UV light irradiation; (b) Kinetic fit for the degradation of CIP with the pure BiPO4 and N-CQDs/BiPO4 materials. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

ESR spectra of the (a, c) DMPO-O2
− and (b, d) DMPO-·OH adducts recorded with (a, b) pure BiPO4 and (c, d) N-CQDs/BiPO4-1 materials under UV light irradiation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) analysis of the pure BiPO4 and N-CQDs/BiPO4-1 materials. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Schematic diagram illustrating the transfer of photogenerated charges of N-CQDs/BiPO4 samples under UV light irradiation along with the possible reaction mechanism. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Tunable oxygen activation induced by oxygen defects in nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots for sustainable boosting photocatalysis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute for Energy Research, Jiangsu University, 301 Xuefu Road, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)/BiPO4 materials have been prepared through an ionic liquid assisted solvothermal method. After the introduction of N-CQDs, the N-CQDs/BiPO4 materials exhibited the increased photocatalytic activity for the degradation of four kinds of colorless pollutants under UV irradiation, such as antibiotic ciprofloxacin, enrofloxacin, tetracycline and phenol 4-chlorophenol. Different from roles of carbon quantum dots previous reported, the improved photocatalytic activity was mainly attributed to the molecular oxygen activation ability of N-CQDs, which derived from the oxygen defects on the surface of N-CQDs. The N-CQDs/BiPO4 materials displayed excellent reusability and stability and demonstrated the oxygen defects in N-CQDs could sustainable activate molecular oxygen for organic pollutant removal under UV irradiation. By the electron spin resonance analysis, the main active species was determined to be superoxide radicals rather than hydroxyl radicals. This study presented some new insight for the mode of action of N-CQDs for the increased photocatalytic activity and also provided an approach to enhance the molecular oxygen activation for photocatalytic pollutant removal or selective organic synthesis.

Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)/BiPO4 materials have been prepared. The improved photocatalytic activity was mainly attributed to the molecular oxygen activation ability of N-CQDs, which derived from the oxygen defects on the surface of N-CQDs.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalysis was regarded as one of the most promising techniques for solving the increasing shortage of energy and the environmental pollution issues [1,2]. Various photocatalysts such as oxides, (oxy)nitrides, elemental photocatalysts and plasmonic photocatalysts have been prepared to achieve the efficient photocatalytic activity [3–7]. Despite these successful achievements, the photocatalytic activity was still need to be further improved to meet the potential industrial applications.

Recently, the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with the size below 10 nm have attracted increasing interest [8–11]. Generally, it possess an amorphous to nanocrystalline core with predominantly sp2 conjugated structure and contain certain oxygen content in the form of various oxygen-containing species such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups [12]. Due to the outstanding physical and chemical properties such as robust chemical inertness, low toxicity, easy functionalization, electron reservoir properties and up-converted photoluminescence behavior, it has been employed for a wide range of applications such as drug delivery, electrocatalysis, photodynamic therapy and chemical sensing [13]. Very recently, CQDs has been introduced into photocatalysis field to improve the photocatalytic activity of the main body photocatalyst, including C3N4 [14], TiO2 [15], Fe2O3 [16], Cu2O [17], Bi2WO6 [18], Bi2MoO6 [19], BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I) [20–22] and so on. However, the mechanism of increased activity for the aforementioned systems exhibited considerable difference. For example, Kang's group found that the introduction of CQDs could greatly increase the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4 for water splitting and considered that the CQDs mainly served as efficient catalyst for H2O2 decomposition [14]. Reisner's group demonstrated the CQDs could act as excellent photosensitizers in the CQDs/Ni-bis(diphosphine) catalyst for effective hydrogen production [23]. MacFarlane's group modified the CQDs on the Cu2O for photocatalytic reduction of CO2 into methanol and found that the improved activity was ascribed to charge separation and transfer capabilities of the CQDs [17]. Liu's and Zhu's groups regarded the NIR photocatalytic activity in their systems was derived from the up-converted PL as well as efficient charge separation and transfer properties of CQDs, respectively [24,25]. Therefore, the structure-activity relationships and substance of CQDs for the increased photocatalytic activity were still need to be further explored. Moreover, doping nitrogen into CQDs could efficiently tune the work function of carbon and induce charge delocalization, so as to increase the electron transfer ability [26,27]. Therefore, if N-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) was built and modified on semiconductor, the superior photocatalytic activity was expected.

As one of the oxyacid salt photocatalysts, bismuth phosphate (BiPO4) has attracted substantial research interest due to its stable chemical structure, nontoxicity, exceptional electronic and optical properties [28,29]. It displayed preferable photocatalytic activity than TiO2 for the degradation of dyes. However, some problems including the low quantum efficiency, less activity sites and high charge carrier recombination rate limited the further application of BiPO4 materials [30]. Up to now, several materials have been employed to couple with BiPO4 to increase the photocatalytic activity, such as semiconductors like BiOI [31], BiVO4 [32], AgBr [33], CdS [34], g-C3N4 [35], noble metal Ag [36] or couple with graphene [37]. However, it was difficult for the large-size materials build perfect interfaces between each other and the electron-hole pairs cannot be separated effectively. Thus, if the small size N-CQDs was introduced to the BiPO4, the maximum accessible area between N-CQDs and BiPO4 can be acquired and may resulted in high-efficiency photocatalytic activity. To the best of our knowledge, there has no report regarding N-CQDs/BiPO4 materials or their applications in the photocatalysis field.

Herein, the novel N-CQDs/BiPO4 materials was prepared via a facile ionic liquid [Omim]H2PO4 assisted solvothermal method. The structure, morphology, optical property and photoelectric properties were studied in details. The photocatalytic activity of N-CQDs/BiPO4 materials was evaluated by the degradation of colorless antibacterial agent ciprofloxacin (CIP), enrofloxacin (ENR), tetracycline (TC) and phenol 4-chlorophenol (4-CP) under UV irradiation, respectively. The relationships between the structure and photocatalytic activity was explored through multiple technique. Some new insight regarding the mechanism of action of N-CQDs for the increased photocatalytic performance was proposed.

All chemicals used were analytical grade reagents without further purification. The ionic liquid 1-octyl-3-methylimidazolium dihydrogen phosphate [Omim]H2PO4 was purchased from Shanghai Chengjie Chemical Co., Ltd. The N-CQDs aqueous solution was prepared according to the literature with some modifications [38]. In details, 5 mmol ammonium citrate was dissolved into 10 mL water and 335 μL ethylenediamine was then added. Then the solution was transferred into 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After the solution was cooled down to room temperature, the obtained product was subjected to dialysis for 24 h to acquire the final N-CQDs aqueous solution.

Fabrication of N-CQDs/BiPO4 materials: 1 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was dissolved in 15 mL of 0.1 M mannitol to obtain solution A; 2 mmol [Omim]H2PO4 was dissolved in a certain amount of N-CQDs aqueous solution and the solution was topped up to 5 mL by distilled water to gain a transparent solution B. Then the solution B was added into solution A under stirring and stirred for further 30 min. The mixture was transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, sealed and heated at 160 °C for 24 h. After cooled down to room temperature, the mixture was centrifuged, washed with distilled water and absolute alcohol for several times. Based on the addition of 0, 0.3, 1 and 3 mL N-CQDs aqueous solution, the prepared N-CQDs/BiPO4 materials were named as BiPO4, N-CQDs/BiPO4-0.3, N-CQDs/BiPO4-1 and N-CQDs/BiPO4-3, respectively.

The crystal structure of the obtained N-CQDs/BiPO4 sample was characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Shimadzu XRD-6000 diffractometer) using Cu Kα irradiation operating at 40 kV and 40 mA. The functional groups were determined by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR, Nexus 470 FT-IR spectrophotometer) using the KBr pellet technique. X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (VG MultiLab 2000) was employed to characterize the chemical composition and valence state of the obtained samples. The morphologies of the N-CQDs/BiPO4 sample were determined by a scanning electron microscope (SEM) and a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL-JEM-2100). UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of the samples were recorded using Shimadzu UV2450 spectrophotometer. The specific surface area and pore volume were measured by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method on the basis of N2 adsorption-desorption measurements (TriStar II 3020 Surface Area and Porosity Analyzer) at 77 K. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the obtained N-CQDs/BiPO4 samples were detected by a Varian Cary Eclipse spectrometer. The electron spin resonance (ESR) signals of spin-trapped radicals were recorded on a Bruker model ESR JES-FA200 spectrometer using spin-trap reagent 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) in methanol and water, respectively. The electrochemical experimental results were recorded on an electrochemical system (CHI 660B) with a standard three electrode system. The N-CQDs/BiPO4/ITO was acted as a working electrode, an Ag/AgCl/sat. KCl electrode as reference electrode and a platinum wire as a counter electrode.

Photocatalytic degradation processes of antibacterial agent ciprofloxacin (CIP), enrofloxacin (ENR), tetracycline (TC) and phenol 4-chlorophenol (4-CP) on N-CQDs/BiPO4 samples were performed to evaluate their photocatalytic performance. A 250 W high pressure Hg lamp was utilized as UV light source. 30 mg of the N-CQDs/BiPO4 samples was added into an aqueous solution of CIP (100 mL, 10 mg/L), ENR (100 mL, 10 mg/L), TC (100 mL, 20 mg/L), 4-CP (100 mL, 10 mg/L), respectively and the mixture was stirred in the dark for 30 min to ensure adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Aeration was carried out using an air pump to guarantee a constant supply of oxygen. After certain intervals, 3 mL aliquot samples were taken and centrifuged to separate the supernatant liquid from photocatalysts. The concentrations of CIP, ENR, TC and 4-CP were monitored using an UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2450) according to its absorbance at 276, 273, 356, and 225 nm respectively.

The crystal structure of the obtained N-CQDs/BiPO4 materials was studied by recording the XRD pattern (Fig. 1 ). The peaks at 2θ = 19.1°, 21.4°, 25.3°, 27.1°, 29.1°, 31.2°, 34.5°, 36.8°, 38.6°, 41.4°, 42.8°, 46.3°, 52.1° and 52.8° represent (011), (−111), (111), (200), (120), (012), (−202), (112), (022), (−130), (−131), (212), (−322), and (040) crystal planes of the monoclinic BiPO4 (JCPDS card No. 15-0767), respectively. There has no obvious characteristic peak for N-CQDs at 26° can be found due to its low content and high dispersion in the composite materials. To determine the existence of N-CQDs in the N-CQDs/BiPO4 materials, the FT-IR and XPS analysis was performed, and the result was shown in Fig. S1, S2.

The SEM and TEM measurements were carried out to characterize the morphology and microstructure of the obtained samples. The SEM demonstrated the rod-like structure of BiPO4 (Fig. S3). The microstructure of N-CQDs/BiPO4 material was further determined by the TEM analysis and the result was shown in Fig. 2 . The rod-like structure can also be seen which was accordance to the SEM results (Fig. 2a). It can be clearly observed that the N-CQDs were uniformly dispersed on the surface of BiPO4 rod-like structure (Fig. 2b and c). From the HR-TEM image (Fig. 2d), the lattice spacing observed for the BiPO4 was measured to be 0.314 nm, corresponding to the (002) crystallographic plane of monoclinic BiPO4 (JCPDS card No. 15-0767). And the dark dots of N-CQDs with the lattice fringe spacing of 0.212 nm can also be seen, which is consistent with the (100) facet of the graphite [39]. EDS result suggested the N-CQDs content was about 0.96 wt% in the N-CQDs/BiPO4-1 material (Fig. S4). The DRS and BET results were shown in Fig. S5 and S6.

The charge separation efficiency was a key factor to determining the performance of photocatalytic process essentially. In order to investigate the separation of photogenerated charge carriers, the PL spectra of the as-prepared samples were measured. As shown in Fig. 3 , the pure BiPO4 displayed a strong PL intensity, signifying the high recombination of photo-generated electron-hole pairs in pure BiPO4 materials. After the N-CQDs was modified on the BiPO4, the PL intensity of the N-CQDs/BiPO4-1 materials greatly decreased which revealing the recombination of photo-generated charge carriers has been suppressed efficiently. The N-CQDs possesses the conjugated π structure, which could facilitate the charge transfer from BiPO4 to N-CQDs and enabled the effective separation of charge carriers. The further analysis of charge separation was performed by electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) analysis and transient photocurrent (Fig. S7).

Antibiotics have been widely used to cure the bacterial infections in human and animal, while the excess use has arouse considerable concern, especially in China. It was reported that 162000 tons of antibiotics has been consumed in 2013 within China and a significant percentage of these antibiotics were discharged as the active form pharmaceutically, which would result in antibiotic resistance within native bacterial populations or direct damage to human body health [40]. Antibiotics have been regarded as one group of emerging environment contaminants due to their negative impacts on ecosystem and human health [41]. It was significant to found appropriate method to remove the antibiotics. In this system, the N-CQDs/BiPO4 materials have been employed for the removal of colorless fluoroquinolones antibiotics CIP under light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the direct photodegradation experiment revealing CIP was stabilized under the UV irradiation with only 4.9% can be decomposed after 120 min. When the BiPO4 was added, 64.4% of CIP can be degraded within 120 min under UV irradiation. After the N-CQDs was introduced to the BiPO4, the photocatalytic activity for CIP degradation increased and the N-CQDs/BiPO4-1 materials displayed the highest activity. 87.5% of CIP can be degraded over N-CQDs/BiPO4-1 after 120 min irradiation. The photocatalytic degradation kinetics of CIP over N-CQDs/BiPO4 materials was studied, and the results were shown in Fig. 4b. The changes of the CIP concentration versus the reaction time for the N-CQDs/BiPO4 materials followed a pseudo-first-order kinetics plot. The N-CQDs/BiPO4-1 materials has the maximum rate constant of 0.0165 min−1, which was about 2 times higher than that of pure BiPO4 (Table S1). The result of total organic carbon experiment for the removal of CIP was shown in Fig. S8. At the same time, the photocatalytic activity of N-CQDs/BiPO4 was further evaluated by the removal of ENR, TC and 4-CP (Fig. S9). Fig. S10 demonstrated the excellent reusability and stability of the N-CQDs/BiPO4 materials.

To gain further insight into the active species, the ESR spin-trap tests with DMPO were carried out in methanol and H2O, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the characteristic signals of DMPO-O2
− can be observed for the pure BiPO4, implying the O2
− can be generated over BiPO4 under UV irradiation. At the same time, the fourfold characteristic peak with an intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 can also be found over BiPO4, corresponding to the DMPO-·OH (Fig. 5b) [42]. It was worth noting that the peak intensity of DMPO-O2
− greatly improved after the N-CQDs was modified on the BiPO4 (Fig. 5c). While the peak intensity of DMPO-·OH generated over N-CQDs/BiPO4-1 materials was similar to that of pure BiPO4 (Fig. 5d). Since the photodegradation processes of organic compounds always utilize active species ·OH and O2
−, the higher concentration of active species may bring about higher photocatalytic degradation activity. Through the comparison of ESR result of pure BiPO4 and N-CQDs/BiPO4-1 materials, it can be deduced that the higher O2
− concentration generated by the N-CQDs/BiPO4-1 resulted in the enhanced photodegradation activity. How can the introduction of N-CQDs could bring about the higher O2
− concentration while the ·OH concentration was nearly constant? The intrinsic reasons need to be clarified.

The XPS valence spectra was first employed to study the total density of states of the valence band (VB) for the BiPO4 and N-CQDs/BiPO4 materials [43]. In BiPO4, the valence band maximum (VBM) value was measured to be 2.56 eV by XPS valence spectra (Fig. S12). According to the band gap obtained from the DRS analysis (4.11 eV), the conduction band minimum was determined to be −1.55 eV. By the comparison with redox potentials for ·OH/OH− and O2/O2
− (+2.38 eV for ·OH/OH− and −0.046 eV for O2/O2
− vs normal hydrogen electrode) [44], the electrons on the CB of BiPO4 was able to reduce O2 to O2
− and the holes on the VB of BiPO4 could oxidize the OH− to ·OH. This was in good agreement with the ESR analysis. With respect to the N-CQDs/BiPO4-1 materials, the similar VBM value can be observed (2.56 eV), indicating the introduction of N-CQDs to BiPO4 did not change the electronic structure of BiPO4 (Fig. S12). It can be deduced that the electronic structure variation was not the main factor to change the O2
− concentration. Consider the production pathway of O2
− was the single electron to active the adsorbed molecular oxygen, the difference of generated O2
− amount may come from the difference of molecular oxygen adsorption process on the surface of BiPO4 and N-CQDs/BiPO4-1 materials. As is well-known, the oxygen interacts weakly with fully oxidized surface, while it was prone to charged by the localized electrons at oxygen defects on the material surface [45]. Based on the previous reports, a large population of diverse surface defect sites can be found on the surface of CQDs [12,13]. Therefore, the molecular oxygen could be easier activated via the N-CQDs in this system. To further confirm this viewpoint, the low-temperature electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) analysis was performed. The N-CQDs sample could display the typical signal at g = 2.001 which corresponded to the oxygen defects, as shown in Fig. S13. Since the existence of different oxygen-containing groups on the N-CQDs, it was reasonable to deduce that the observed defects were oxygen defects. It can be seen from Fig. 6 that the typical signal corresponded to the oxygen defects cannot be found in pure BiPO4, while the modification of N-CQDs bring about the obvious signal. Through the EPR analysis, it can be found that the molecular oxygen can be better activated by the introduction of N-CQDs, thus more O2
− amount can be generated by N-CQDs/BiPO4-1 materials and improved photocatalytic activity was obtained. This result was in accordance with the analysis above mentioned. Through the cyclic experiment, it demonstrated that the N-CQDs could sustainably activate molecular oxygen for organic pollutant removal. This was attributed to that the oxygen defects in the N-CQDs could be effectively refreshed by the UV light irradiation [45].

From the DRS and BET analysis, it can be concluded that the light absorption and BET surface area were not the main factors to impact the photocatalytic performance in this system. In some literatures, the up-converted PL of CQDs was considered as the main factor to increase the photocatalytic activity [22,23]. Therefore, a control experiment was performed by using N-CQDs/BiPO4 materials as photocatalyst to explore the photocatalytic activity for CIP degradation under visible light. However, the negligible degradation activity can be found for the CIP, revealing the up-converted PL may not play the crucial role for the improved activity in this system. A possible photocatalytic mechanism diagram of N-CQDs/BiPO4-1 materials was presented in Fig. 7 based on the above analysis. When the UV light was irradiated on the N-CQDs/BiPO4 materials, the electrons on the VB of BiPO4 was excited to the CB and leaving the holes on the VB of BiPO4. The electrons would transfer from the CB of BiPO4 to the N-CQDs and enabled the spatial separation of the photogenerated charge carriers. Then the electrons on the N-CQDs could activate the O2 to yield O2
− via single electron reduction. And the holes on the VB of BiPO4 would oxidize OH− to produce ·OH. The greatly enhanced O2
− concentration of N-CQDs/BiPO4 materials compared with pure BiPO4 resulted in the improved photodegradation activity for CIP, ENR, TC and 4-CP.

In conclusion, the N-CQDs/BiPO4 materials has been controlled prepared via a solvothermal process with the N-CQDs modified on the surface of BiPO4. The N-CQDs/BiPO4 materials displayed improved photocatalytic activity for the degradation of different pollutants such as CIP, ENR, TC and 4-CP. The increased O2
− concentration generated by the N-CQDs/BiPO4 materials was responsible for the higher photocatalytic performance. The oxygen defects on the N-CQDs was demonstrated to be able to enable the better activation of molecular oxygen. These findings gain deep insight into the activity strengthening mechanism by the N-CQDs modification.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21476098, 21471069 and 21576123), the Doctoral Innovation Fund of Jiangsu Province (KYZZ16_0340) and the Science and Technology support program of Zhenjiang (SH2014018).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2016.12.030.

A chemical method for identifying the photocatalytic active sites on carbon dots

Carbon quantum dots and their applications
Metal-free efficient photocatalyst for stable visible water splitting via a two-electron pathway
Carbon quantum dots/TiO2 composites for efficient photocatalytic hydrogen evolution
Hybrid heterostructures based on hematite and highly hydrophilic carbon dots with photocatalytic activity
Carbon quantum dots/Cu2O heterostructures for solar-light-driven conversion of CO2 to methanol

The synergistic role of carbon quantum dots for the improved photocatalytic performance of Bi2MoO6

Ionic liquid-induced strategy for carbon quantum dots/BiOX (X = Br, Cl) hybrid nanosheets with superior visible light-driven photocatalysis
Carbon quantum dots in situ coupling to bismuth oxyiodide via reactable ionic liquid with enhanced photocatalytic molecular oxygen activation performance
Solar hydrogen production using carbon quantum dots and a molecular nickel catalyst

Hierarchical carbon quantum dots/hydrogenated-γ-TaON heterojunctions for broad spectrum photocatalytic performance
N-doped carbon quantum dots for TiO2-based photocatalysts and dye-sensitized solar cells

Enhancement of photocatalytic activity for BiPO4 via phase junction

Enhancement of visible light mineralization ability and photocatalytic activity of BiPO4/BiOI via charge transfer
A highly efficient UV-Vis-NIR active Ln3+-doped BiPO4/BiVO4 nanocomposite for photocatalysis
Synthesis, characterization and photocatalytic property of AgBr/BiPO4 heterojunction photocatalyst
Enhanced visible-light photocatalytic degradation of methyl orange by BiPO4-CdS composites synthesized using a microwave-assisted method
Dramatic activity of C3N4/BiPO4 photocatalyst with core/shell structure formed by self-assembly

Role of graphene on the surface chemical reactions of BiPO4-rGO with low OH-related defects
Highly luminescent nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as effective fluorescent probes for mercuric and iodide ions
Carbon quantum dots with photo enhanced hydrogen-bond catalytic activity in aldol condensations
Comprehensive evaluation of antibiotics emission and fate in the river basins of China: source analysis, multimedia modeling, and linkage to bacterial resistance
Antibiotics in drinking water in shanghai and their contribution to antibiotic exposure of school children
Formation of Bi2WO6 bipyramids with vacancy pairs for enhanced solar-driven photoactivity
Increasing the visible light absorption of graphitic carbon nitride (melon) photocatalysts by homogeneous self-modification with nitrogen vacancies
Reactable ionic liquid-assisted rapid synthesis of BiOI hollow microspheres at room temperature with enhanced photocatalytic activity
Sustainable molecular oxygen activation with oxygen vacancies on the {001} facets of BiOCl nanosheets under solar light
